meaning: Publication-significant Lean-complete proofs AutoMath has definitively found and proved in Lean.

proofs.title: Determine the exact value of R(B8, B10)

verification.strongest_honest_claim: Lean-complete packet for R(B8, B10) = 37: the preserved verified proof reduces any 37-vertex obstruction to srg(37,18,7,10), and Lean now proves that terminal strongly regular graph cannot exist via the cubic annihilator, eigenvalue count, trace, and trace-square contradiction.

claim.question: Is R(B8, B10) = 37 or 38?
claim.canonical_statement: Determine the exact value of R(B8, B10).
claim.intended_statement: Either prove that every graph on 37 vertices contains B8 or its complement contains B10 and thus show R(B8, B10) = 37, or construct a 37-vertex graph avoiding B8 whose complement avoids B10 and thus show R(B8, B10) = 38.
claim.candidate_theorem_slice: Lean-sealed terminal slice: no strongly regular graph with parameters (37,18,7,10) exists. Combined with the verified Goodman-saturation reduction, this seals the R(B8, B10) = 37 packet.

paper_context.canonical_source: Bernard Lidicky, Gwen McKinley, Florian Pfender, and Steven Van Overberghe, "Small Ramsey Numbers for Books, Wheels, and Generalizations" (Electronic Journal of Combinatorics 32(4), 2025), Theorem 1; Stanislaw P. Radziszowski, "Small Ramsey Numbers" (Electronic Journal of Combinatorics, Dynamic Survey DS1.17, 2024), Section 5.3(g); William J. Wesley, "Lower bounds for book Ramsey numbers" (Discrete Mathematics 349(5), 2026), abstract-level status check; and bounded exact-term, canonical-source, outside-source, and recent-status web checks performed on 2026-04-13.
paper_context.literature_gap: Current public sources support only the one-gap window 37 <= R(B8, B10) <= 38, and the bounded 2026-04-13 audit did not uncover a later exact determination.
paper_context.publication_packet_frontier_basis: The 2025 book-Ramsey paper implies the current lower bound 37 <= R(B8, B10) through its almost-diagonal theorem, while the older Rousseau-Sheehan upper-bound mechanism recorded in DS1.17 still gives R(B8, B10) <= 38. The sampled 2026 Wesley follow-up advertises new lower bounds for several book pairs and an infinite-family result for R(B_{n-1}, B_n), but it does not advertise an exact resolution of R(B8, B10).
paper_context.publication_packet_literature_scope: Lidicky-McKinley-Pfender-Van Overberghe 2025, DS1.17 Section 5.3(g), Wesley 2026 abstract-level status check, and bounded 2026-04-13 exact-term, canonical-source, outside-source, and recent-status searches for R(B8, B10).
paper_context.prior_work_stop_sentence: Current sources stop at the one-gap window 37 <= R(B8, B10) <= 38.
paper_context.broader_theorem_nonimplication_note: Known exact families settle R(B_{n-1}, B_n) and many diagonal cases, but they do not settle R(B_{n-2}, B_n) for n = 10. The 2026 follow-up advertises infinite-family progress only for the former line, so the B8/B10 slice is not already forced by a broader published theorem.

paper_shape.paper_title_hint: The Exact Value of R(B8, B10)
paper_shape.hypothetical_title: The Exact Value of R(B8, B10)
paper_shape.hypothetical_abstract: We determine the book Ramsey number R(B8, B10). Existing work leaves this almost-diagonal case in the one-gap window 37 <= R(B8, B10) <= 38. Our result closes a natural residue in the recent book-Ramsey program around the almost-diagonal family R(B_{n-2}, B_n).
paper_shape.paper_shape: A one-theorem exact-value note on an almost-diagonal book Ramsey residue.
paper_shape.publication_if_solved: Closing the one-gap case R(B8, B10) would already read as the title theorem of a short note on almost-diagonal book Ramsey numbers.
paper_shape.single_solve_paper_explanation: The exact value is already the honest title theorem, and the surrounding literature has done most of the setup. A successful solve leaves only proof polishing, comparison with neighboring solved book pairs, and a short remark on the boundary of the known family theorems.
paper_shape.publication_packet_near_paper_reason: The verified proof supplies the title theorem; remaining work is exposition, reference polish, and optional Lean formal sealing.

lemmas: Theorem 1 of the 2025 book-Ramsey paper gives the lower bound 37 <= R(B8, B10).
DS1.17 Section 5.3(g) gives the generic upper bound R(Bm, Bn) <= 2(m + n + 1); for (m, n) = (8, 10) this is R(B8, B10) <= 38.
The same 2025 paper proves the neighboring exact value R(B9, B10) = 39, which gives a local benchmark one step closer to the diagonal.
transfer_kit.toy_example: The exact case R(B9, B10) = 39 is the nearest solved almost-diagonal example showing how the 4n - 1 line behaves one notch above the target.
transfer_kit.known_obstruction: A 37-vertex obstruction would have to be a rigid srg(37,18,7,10), which the verified spectral trace argument rules out.
transfer_kit.prior_work_stop_sentence: Current sources stop at the one-gap window 37 <= R(B8, B10) <= 38.
transfer_kit.recommended_first_attack: Already solved in the preserved proof packet: use Goodman saturation to force the SRG obstruction and then discharge it spectrally.
transfer_kit.paper_if_solved: The paper is a short exact-value note closing an almost-diagonal book Ramsey residue.

proof_package.publication_packet_artifact_requirements: Verified proof that every 37-vertex graph forces B8 or a complement B10, plus preserved solve, verify, publication-audit, and Lean-support artifacts.
proof_package.next_action: Lean-complete: preserve the proof artifacts, keep this packet out of lean_queue.json, and use lean_complete.json as the formal-seal registry entry.

formalization.lean_gate_reason: Lean completed the packet-sealing SRG obstruction: AutoMath.RB8B10BookRamsey.no_srg_37_18_7_10 proves that no srg(37,18,7,10) exists, closing the previously missing spectral/minpoly bridge.
formalization.axiom_audit_note: Checked with the AutoMath Lean/mathlib backend; no sorry/admit placeholders in the backend or mirrored artifact file.

verification.strongest_honest_claim: Lean-complete packet for the cyclic (1925,260,35) row: the verified multiplier and contraction reductions force a C_175 orbit-refinement system, and Lean proves that this system has no natural-number solution. Therefore no cyclic (1925,260,35) difference set exists, subject to the bounded 2026-04-16 prior-art audit.

claim.question: Does the cyclic group C_1925 admit a (1925,260,35)-difference set?
claim.canonical_statement: Determine whether the cyclic group C_1925 admits a (1925,260,35)-difference set.
claim.intended_statement: Determine whether the cyclic group C_1925 admits a (1925,260,35)-difference set.
claim.candidate_theorem_slice: Lean-sealed terminal slice: no natural-number coefficients on the eight multiplication-by-3 orbits of C_175 can satisfy the forced projections to C_25 and C_7 together with the required square-sum identity.

paper_context.canonical_source: Leonard D. Baumert and Daniel M. Gordon, "On the existence of cyclic difference sets with small parameters" (Fields Institute Communications 41, 2004), especially Table 2 listing the exact cyclic row (1925,260,35) among the possible cases with 150 <= k <= 300 and gcd(v,n) > 1.
paper_context.literature_gap: Baumert-Gordon stop at listing (1925,260,35) as a remaining possible cyclic case in Table 2; the capped 2026-04-16 audit found no later exact-tuple settlement.
paper_context.publication_packet_frontier_basis: Baumert-Gordon Table 2 isolates (1925,260,35) as an exact remaining cyclic row after the source's standard eliminations.
paper_context.publication_packet_literature_scope: Baumert-Gordon 2004 Table 2 and its Section 2-3 machinery, bounded exact/alternate tuple web probes on 2026-04-16, Dan Gordon's publications/database surfaces, and local attempt/search/paper/failed memory.
paper_context.prior_work_stop_sentence: Baumert and Gordon stop at listing (1925,260,35) as a remaining possible cyclic case in Table 2.
paper_context.broader_theorem_nonimplication_note: Baumert-Gordon explicitly leave (1925,260,35) in Table 2 after their standard eliminations, so the row is not already dispatched inside the canonical source. The bounded 2026-04-16 web audit did not surface a later exact-tuple theorem settling this parameter set.

paper_shape.paper_title_hint: Nonexistence of a Cyclic (1925,260,35) Difference Set
paper_shape.hypothetical_abstract: We determine whether the cyclic group C_1925 admits a (1925,260,35)-difference set. Baumert and Gordon isolate this exact parameter row in Table 2 as one of the residual cyclic cases with 150 <= k <= 300 and gcd(v,n) > 1. A direct solution closes a named survivor in a standard source and would support a short note with only light framing left after the proof.
paper_shape.paper_shape: A short residual-case note closing one exact cyclic Table 2 survivor by proof or disproof.
paper_shape.publication_if_solved: Settling the cyclic (1925,260,35) row would plausibly yield a short residual-case note whose title theorem is exactly this Baumert-Gordon Table 2 survivor.
paper_shape.single_solve_paper_explanation: The source already supplies a crisp theorem statement, a canonical table entry, and the standard necessary conditions that failed to eliminate the case. That means a single proof or disproof would already provide most of the eventual note, with only a brief introduction, the cleaned main argument, and a short literature-status paragraph left to write. This is not just a tiny curiosity because it closes one exact residual row in a recognized cyclic-difference-set classification table.
paper_shape.publication_packet_near_paper_reason: If the row is still open, one decisive proof or disproof already supplies the title theorem, the canonical literature anchor, and nearly all of the note's mathematical payload.

lemmas: Baumert-Gordon Table 2 isolates (1925,260,35) as an exact remaining cyclic row with gcd(v,n) > 1.
Section 2 records the standard arithmetic and multiplier filters already exhausted before the tuple reaches Table 2.
Theorem 3.1 gives contracted coefficient equations for every divisor w of v.
Theorem 3.2 gives multiplier-orbit equalities once a suitable prime-power divisor of n yields a contracted multiplier modulo a divisor of v.
transfer_kit.toy_example: Contract a hypothetical 260-set modulo 25 or 35 and compare the resulting coefficient vector with the orbit sizes forced by powers of 3 acting on the quotient.
transfer_kit.known_obstruction: Any surviving configuration must satisfy both the Table 2 residual arithmetic and a rigid contracted-multiplier pattern across the 5^2 * 7 * 11 factorization of v.
transfer_kit.prior_work_stop_sentence: Baumert and Gordon stop at listing (1925,260,35) as a remaining possible cyclic case in Table 2.
transfer_kit.recommended_first_attack: Choose a divisor of 1925 on which a prime divisor of n induces a useful contracted multiplier, then intersect the forced orbit decomposition with the Theorem 3.1 coefficient equations.
transfer_kit.paper_if_solved: If solved exactly, the paper would be a short note closing the cyclic Table 2 row (1925,260,35).

proof_package.publication_packet_artifact_requirements: A proof or disproof for C_1925, the decisive multiplier-orbit or contraction argument, and a short status note recording the bounded 2026-04-16 outside-source audit.
proof_package.next_action: Lean-complete: remove this packet from lean_queue.json, preserve the proof artifacts, and use lean_complete.json as the formal-seal registry entry.

formalization.lean_gate_reason: Lean completed the direct packet-sealing theorem slice: AutoMath.CyclicDifferenceSet192526035.no_compatible_c175_refinement proves that the verified reduced C_175 orbit-refinement system has no natural-number solution.
formalization.lean_statement: AutoMath.CyclicDifferenceSet192526035.intendedStatement: no natural-number solution to the forced C_175 orbit-refinement system.
formalization.axiom_audit_note: Checked with the AutoMath Lean/mathlib backend; no sorry, admit, axiom, or unsafe placeholders in the backend or mirrored artifact file.
